failure: The built-in sample project opened with a tour overlay that blocked the main workspace.
cause: NVivo displayed a first-run/tutorial window above the project content.
lesson: When opening the sample project, explicitly dismiss the tour before validating the ribbon, navigation pane, or coding workflow.

failure: Once a project was open, raw UI Automation exposed very few meaningful control names inside the main workspace.
cause: NVivo's in-project panes on this machine are only weakly surfaced through UI Automation, even though start-screen buttons and wizards are accessible.
lesson: Do not rely on control-name scraping alone for deep NVivo project work. Prefer screenshot/OCR-capable computer-use tools, human-supervised desktop control, and stepwise verification.

failure: The sample project window continued to show a loading-content pane long after project launch.
cause: NVivo mixes asynchronous content loading with Chromium-hosted panes, so UI readiness does not track process launch alone.
lesson: After opening a project, wait for overlays to clear and confirm workspace readiness before assuming the interface is stable. Distinguish project-open success from workspace-ready success.

failure: Start-screen actions such as FILE or Log In changed state without always producing a new top-level window within a short wait.
cause: Some NVivo actions appear to update embedded panes rather than creating separate windows.
lesson: For NVivo start-screen validation, treat state change and control availability as evidence, not only new-window creation.

failure: Guide terminology did not match the live UI labels exactly during navigation validation.
cause: The guide used `Nodes`, while the current NVivo 14 project UI exposed `Codes` through automation-visible labels; other query/help paths also vary by release documentation.
lesson: Record live UI labels as evidence and treat older/newer documentation paths as approximate. Prefer validated shortcuts and current on-screen labels over memory.

failure: Navigating to the queries area with keyboard shortcuts did not yield a clean `Queries` label in the visible named controls.
cause: The current workspace exposes sparse names and may surface subareas like `Static Sets` instead of the broader container name.
lesson: When validating NVivo navigation, accept indirect evidence such as known shortcuts, changed visible labels, and workspace focus shifts rather than expecting every section header to appear explicitly.

failure: Typing a disposable code name after `CTRL+Q` in editable mode inserted text into the document heading instead of the quick-coding field.
cause: Input focus was not actually in the quick-coding bar even though the shortcut was expected to move it there.
lesson: Never type into NVivo quick-coding workflows without visual focus confirmation. In this environment, `CTRL+Q` is not safe to trust blindly inside the sample project.

failure: Clicking what looked like the bottom `Code to` input area still resulted in text being written into the open document.
cause: The document editing surface retained focus, and the visible bottom placeholder was not enough proof that keyboard input would route there.
lesson: Visible placeholders are not sufficient evidence of focus in NVivo. Confirm actual caret placement or use safer creation paths before typing code names.

failure: Opening `Overview of Sample Project` repeatedly triggered a `Waiting for printer connection...` modal that blocked progress.
cause: The sample file appears to reference print-related content or environment-dependent resources during open.
lesson: Treat printer-connection popups as a standard blocker in this sample project. Dismiss them before assuming the document is ready for coding or query actions.

failure: Attempts to recover from bad input with undo did not fully restore the document to an untouched state in one step.
cause: The mistyped text changed heading content and spacing in ways that were only partially reversed by `CTRL+Z`.
lesson: After failed automation in an editable NVivo document, prefer closing or force-reopening without saving rather than trusting partial undo as a clean recovery.

failure: NVivo repeatedly showed `Waiting for printer connection...` when opening sample content.
cause: Windows default printer was `EPSON WF-2860 Series`, and Windows automatic default printer management was enabled.
lesson: Disable Windows automatic default printer management by setting `LegacyDefaultPrinterMode=1`, set `Microsoft Print to PDF` as default, then restart NVivo and verify sample content opens without the printer modal.

failure: Quick Coding originally failed because text was entered into the document body instead of creating a code.
cause: The workflow did not first establish a real active text selection, so the bottom `Code to` pane was disabled or focus stayed in the document surface.
lesson: Quick Coding requires a verified active selection before typing. Use Ctrl+A or a screenshot-verified drag selection, confirm the bottom `Code to` pane is enabled, click into it, verify focus bounds are in the bottom field, paste the code name, then press Enter. Validate by source `Codes` and `References` counts incrementing and the new code appearing in the Codes panel.

failure: Needed a stable route to create a code without relying on Quick Coding.
cause: Quick Coding is useful for coding a current selection, but code management should use the Codes panel/ribbon path when possible.
lesson: Use `Create -> Code` from the Codes area. The `New Code` dialog opens with the Name field focused; enter the name and confirm. Validate the new code appears with Files 0 and References 0.

failure: Trying to replace an existing quick-coding token with another existing code name appended the pasted text instead of replacing the token.
cause: The bottom `Code to` field behaves like a token/custom combo field, not a normal text box. Backspace and Ctrl+A did not reliably clear the old token.
lesson: Do not use the bottom token field to switch existing codes unless the old token is visibly cleared. Prefer creating/coding a new quick code or use another explicit existing-code workflow once validated.

failure: Dragging selected source text onto an existing code row did not increment the target code's Files/References counts.
cause: The drag likely began from the embedded text surface but did not carry the selection payload onto the code row, or the modal save reminder interrupted the operation.
lesson: Do not treat drag-to-existing-code as validated yet. It needs another run with no save reminder active, a shorter selection, and a clear drop hover state before release.

failure: A `Save Reminder` appeared during NVivo training and was initially treated as an interruption to dismiss.
cause: The run was disposable sample-project training, but this behavior would be unsafe during real coding work because NVivo and other legacy desktop tools can crash during long sessions or large-file handling.
lesson: For real NVivo projects, treat `Save Reminder` as a safety checkpoint: save progress before continuing. Only dismiss without saving during explicitly disposable experiments. After any unexpected close/crash, verify NVivo process state, reopen the project, and confirm recent codes/references/memos still exist.
